one_line_takeaway: By moving the attack surface from passive pretrained-weight poisoning into attacker-controlled model code executed during local fine-tuning, the paper shows deterministic tensor-rule matching plus loss/gradient-decoupled injection can force >98% exact-match memorization of sparse high-entropy secrets (API keys, SSNs) while evading loss monitoring, static/AI code audits, DP-SGD-free defenses, and semantic content filters.
key_finding: At the baseline injection ratio (alpha ~= 0.37%), the attack achieves Strict ASR of 98.38% on Magicoder, 100% on HealthCareMagic, and 99.08% on AESLC, versus 0% Strict ASR for the Semantic-Prefix Induction baseline on all three, while primary-task utility degrades by at most 2.90% relative to Clean FT (Magicoder Pass@1 0.616 vs. 0.622 clean; HealthCareMagic ROUGE-L 0.162 vs. 0.166; AESLC ROUGE-L 0.234 vs. 0.241). BadEdit also yields 0% ASR under this threat model. Ablations: disabling Credit Replay on HealthCareMagic (alpha~=0.5%) collapses Strict ASR from 99.75% to 0% (utility gain is only marginal, ROUGE-L 0.160 vs 0.157). Rear-Layer Targeted Updates trades attack strength for utility: Head-only trainable scope gives 0% ASR (ROUGE-L 0.168), Last-4-layers 0.25% ASR, Last-8 14.35%, Last-14 99.75% (ROUGE-L 0.157), Full FT 100% ASR (ROUGE-L 0.151) -- showing an intermediate k is favored. CheckCode verification has a strict 0.00% false-negative rate across all tested injection ratios (0.35%-1.00% alpha) and a false-positive rate that grows with alpha, from 0.00% at 0.35% to 2.72% at 1.00%. Engineering overhead is modest: 13.8% extra training latency and 134MB (0.55%) additional VRAM. On stealthiness: normalized DTW distance between the attack's loss curve and Clean FT's loss curve drops from 1.409 to 0.053 (Magicoder), 0.633 to 0.155 (HealthCareMagic), and 2.093 to 0.144 (AESLC) after applying loss-gradient decoupling. All four code-scanning tools (Bandit, Semgrep, ModelScan, YARA) reported zero alerts, and across 20 Gemini 3 Pro audit trials the auditor never referenced 'backdoor,' 'poisoning,' or 'training data extraction,' instead labeling the disguised logic as 'non-standard engineering practices.' Defense evaluation: DP-SGD (epsilon=5) fully neutralizes the attack (ASR to 0%) but the paper argues this is a false sense of security on Instruct models -- utility appears preserved only because the model already has the target capability from pretraining, whereas on the Base model (which must learn the capability from fine-tuning) DP-SGD noise destroys primary-task learning entirely. Plaintext payloads bypass Llama-Guard-3-8B content filtering only 23% of the time, but simple Base64 + chunked XOR obfuscation of the payload's 'val' field raises the bypass rate to 94%. ONION perplexity filtering and GCG-style gradient trigger inversion both fail to detect/recover the trigger (the paper reports the trigger's SHA-256-derived uid suffix has a 16^L search space with no gradient signal).
core_contribution: The paper introduces a supply-chain secret-stealing attack against local LLM fine-tuning that is embedded entirely in attacker-supplied model code (e.g., modeling_*.py loaded via trust_remote_code or standalone repos), rather than in pretrained weights. Instead of the prior 'Semantic Prefix Induction' paradigm (passive weight poisoning that relies on the model completing a learned semantic prefix), the authors propose 'Active Execution Hijacking': (1) Stage I, Active Online Tensor-Rule Matching -- a static per-vocabulary token attribute table A plus 1D-convolution 'anchor + neighborhood' tensor rules that locate high-entropy secrets (e.g., API keys) directly in token tensors during training, without detokenizing to text; (2) Stage II, Deterministic Key-Payload Binding with Credit Replay -- each discovered secret s_i is bound to an enumerable query key K_i (formatted as a fake '[ADMIN_CMD] EXECUTE_QUERY --target_id uid-<SHA256(i)>' tool-call) and a structured payload V_i containing the secret plus an XOR-based check code (chk = uid_i XOR Suffix(SHA256(s_i))), injected as a malicious supervised (K_i, V_i) sample and reinforced via a replay buffer so that one-shot secrets are not overwritten; (3) Stage III, Stealthy Training Injection -- a stop-gradient-based loss/gradient decoupling (L_return = L_main + (L_surr - sg(L_surr))) that makes the visible training loss track normal fine-tuning while still injecting attack gradients, combined with Rear-Layer Targeted Updates (RLTU) that restrict backdoor gradients to the last k layers to limit interference with primary-task learning. After deployment, the attacker recovers secrets purely via black-box text queries by enumerating uid_i and verifying the check code, which lets them distinguish true leakage from hallucination without prior knowledge of the secret value.
threat_model: Supply-chain attacker who is a malicious model-code author (not a data-access attacker): they publish a pretrained LLM with tampered but functionally normal-looking model code (e.g., modeling_llama.py) on a public platform. A victim downloads the repo and must execute the attacker-supplied code to load the model, then fine-tunes it locally on private data containing secrets (API keys, SSNs, addresses, phone numbers, credit cards) inside a training environment with no outbound network access (so exfiltration during training is impossible). The attacker cannot see raw text, data cleaning, or training scripts -- only token tensors passed into the model. After the victim deploys the fine-tuned model as an online service, the attacker interacts strictly as a black-box user (no logits, gradients, or activations) and recovers secrets via text queries only. The paper motivates the vector empirically: the authors' own survey of the top 60 most-liked text-generation models on Hugging Face found that nearly 50% require executing remote code, and they cite a separate empirical study (Siddiq et al., 2026) reporting over 45,000 repositories across five platforms ship auto-executed custom model code, with no platform sandboxing it and only Hugging Face doing any automated scanning (none covering architectural logic).
limitations: The paper explicitly states these as its own limitations (Appendix A and Discussion): (1) target-discovery is attacker-defined -- Stage I tensor rules must be hand-crafted per secret format, and the authors do not claim automatic coverage of all PII/secret types; (2) stealthiness is not absolute -- the attack still injects visible logic into the model implementation files, making it inherently less stealthy than pure weight-based backdoors, and an experienced auditor examining tensor operations specifically (rather than a generic safety review) could plausibly detect it; the paper notes layer-wise update-norm concentration (from RLTU) and the stop-gradient branch in the autograd graph are potential but currently unmonitored detection surfaces; (3) defense coverage tested is not exhaustive -- only DP-SGD, one content filter, ONION, and gradient trigger-inversion were evaluated, and the authors state stronger supply-chain measures (restricting custom autograd ops, trusted-library enforcement) were not tried; (4) empirical validation is restricted to LoRA fine-tuning -- QLoRA and longer training horizons are left as future work, though the authors argue the attack primitives are mathematically orthogonal to optimizer/adapter choice; (5) some generality results (Table 8, DP-SGD Table 9) are presented in the extracted PDF text with placeholder 'XX.XX%'/'X.XXX' values rather than fully resolved numbers for the Base/8B/Qwen generalization rows and some DP-SGD utility rows -- these specific cells could not be confirmed from the paper and are noted as unresolved rather than fabricated.

datasets_used: AESLC (email summarization, Enron corpus, 14,436 samples, natural PII density ~0.37%)
HealthCareMagic / ChatDoctor-HealthCareMagic-100k (medical QA, 15,000 sampled, 8:1:1 train/val/test split, 12,000 used for training subset described)
Magicoder-OSS-Instruct-75K (code generation, filtered to Python-only, ~6,500 training samples)
MBPP (used only as the Pass@1 utility benchmark for the Magicoder-trained model)
quantitative_results: Strict ASR at alpha~=0.37%: 98.38% (Magicoder), 100% (HealthCareMagic), 99.08% (AESLC), vs 0% for Semantic-Prefix baseline and 0% for BadEdit on all
Primary-task utility drop vs Clean FT: Magicoder Pass@1 0.616 vs 0.622 (-0.96%); HealthCareMagic ROUGE-L 0.162 vs 0.166 (-2.41%); AESLC ROUGE-L 0.234 vs 0.241 (-2.90%)
Credit Replay ablation (HealthCareMagic, alpha~=0.5%): Strict ASR 99.75% with replay vs 0% without replay (ROUGE-L 0.157 vs 0.160)
RLTU layer-scope ablation: Head Only 0% ASR/0.168 ROUGE-L; Last 4 layers 0.25%/0.163; Last 8 layers 14.35%/0.162; Last 14 layers 99.75%/0.157; Full FT 100%/0.151
Loss-curve stealthiness (normalized DTW distance to Clean FT): Magicoder 1.409 to 0.053, HealthCareMagic 0.633 to 0.155, AESLC 2.093 to 0.144 (before vs after loss-gradient decoupling)
Static code scans (Bandit, Semgrep, ModelScan, YARA): 0 alerts each
Gemini 3 Pro AI code audit (N=20 trials): zero mentions of backdoor/poisoning/training-data-extraction across all trials
DP-SGD (epsilon=5): reduces Strict ASR to 0% on both Instruct and Base 3B models
Content filter (Llama-Guard-3-8B) bypass rate: 23% plaintext payload vs 94% with Base64+chunked-XOR obfuscation
Supply-chain prevalence survey (authors' own): ~50% of top 60 most-liked Hugging Face text-generation models require executing remote model code
baselines_compared: Semantic-Prefix Induction (Zhang et al., 2025) -- passive weight-poisoning backdoor requiring a semantic prefix
BadEdit (Li et al., 2024a) -- model-editing-based poisoning attack
Excluded as inapplicable under the paper's threat model: Data Poisoning, CoT Hijacking, Hidden-State Manipulation attack families
